A foundational course in artificial intelligence covering core concepts, modern techniques, and hands-on practice.

## What you'll learn

This foundational course introduces artificial intelligence from first principles. By the end of the eight weeks, you'll have a working mental model of how modern AI systems are built, trained, and deployed — and you'll have built one yourself.

## Topics

- The mathematics behind machine learning — just enough linear algebra, probability, and calculus to follow what's happening
- Supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning at a conceptual level
- Building and training neural networks with practical tooling
- The transformer architecture and how it underpins modern language models
- Responsible AI: bias, evaluation, and deployment considerations

## Format

Weekly live sessions, asynchronous lectures, hands-on coding labs, and a capstone project completed in pairs.

## Prerequisites

Comfort with Python at an intermediate level. No prior machine-learning experience required.

From neural network basics through transformers — building, training, and reasoning about deep models.

## What you'll learn

This course takes you from the basics of neural networks through the transformer architectures that power today's language and vision models. Emphasis throughout is on building intuition through implementation — you'll write your own networks, train them, and debug what goes wrong.

## Topics

- Forward and backward propagation from scratch
- Convolutional networks and modern computer-vision architectures
- Recurrent networks and the path from RNNs to attention
- The transformer architecture in depth
- Training dynamics, optimisation, and common failure modes

## Prerequisites

The AI Foundations or Machine Learning Fundamentals course (or equivalent prior experience). Comfort with Python and basic linear algebra.

What you’ll learn

This course takes you from the basics of neural networks through the transformer architectures that power today’s language and vision models. Emphasis throughout is on building intuition through implementation — you’ll write your own networks, train them, and debug what goes wrong.

Topics

  • Forward and backward propagation from scratch
  • Convolutional networks and modern computer-vision architectures
  • Recurrent networks and the path from RNNs to attention
  • The transformer architecture in depth
  • Training dynamics, optimisation, and common failure modes

Prerequisites

The AI Foundations or Machine Learning Fundamentals course (or equivalent prior experience). Comfort with Python and basic linear algebra.
A practical introduction to classical machine learning — regression, classification, clustering, and the evaluation methods that hold them up.

## What you'll learn

A grounding in classical machine learning before you reach for deep networks. The course is structured around the core families of ML methods and the evaluation discipline that separates working models from misleading ones.

## Topics

- Linear and logistic regression, decision trees, random forests
- Clustering and dimensionality reduction
- Cross-validation, train/test discipline, and metric selection
- Feature engineering and data preparation
- A first look at when classical methods beat deep learning — and when they don't

## Format

Six weeks of weekly modules, each anchored by a coding notebook and a small dataset to work through.

What you’ll learn

A grounding in classical machine learning before you reach for deep networks. The course is structured around the core families of ML methods and the evaluation discipline that separates working models from misleading ones.

Topics

  • Linear and logistic regression, decision trees, random forests
  • Clustering and dimensionality reduction
  • Cross-validation, train/test discipline, and metric selection
  • Feature engineering and data preparation
  • A first look at when classical methods beat deep learning — and when they don’t

Six weeks of weekly modules, each anchored by a coding notebook and a small dataset to work through.
ChowNet: A Diverse Image Dataset for African Cuisine Recognition

We present ChowNet, an image dataset and benchmark for recognising African cuisine. Existing food-recognition datasets contain little African food imagery, leaving downstream applications poorly suited to billions of meals. ChowNet establishes a baseline and a path forward.

## Motivation

Food-recognition models trained on Western-centric datasets fail systematically on African cuisine. As these models propagate into nutrition tracking, agricultural research, and consumer products, the failure mode quietly excludes a continent's worth of meals from the systems built atop them.

## Dataset

ChowNet collects images across regional cuisines, with annotation guided by community contributors familiar with the dishes catalogued. Each image is labelled with the dish, region of origin, and key ingredients where identifiable.

## Baselines

We benchmark a suite of contemporary vision models — both general-purpose and food-specialised. The performance gap between general food-recognition tasks and ChowNet's task indicates substantial headroom for targeted research.

## Open release

The dataset is released for non-commercial research use. We invite contributions of additional regional cuisines and welcome feedback on annotation conventions.

Towards Speech Recognition for Under-Resourced African Languages: A Sauti Project Benchmark
Proceedings of the African NLP Workshop

We introduce a new benchmark for speech recognition across five under-resourced African languages, alongside baseline results from contemporary self-supervised speech models. Our work highlights the gap between high-resource and low-resource performance and proposes evaluation protocols suited to the linguistic diversity of the continent.

## Background

Speech recognition has made dramatic progress on a small set of high-resource languages, but performance on the thousands of African languages spoken by hundreds of millions of people lags substantially. This paper introduces a benchmark intended to track that gap and accelerate its closing.

## Contributions

- A curated evaluation set spanning five African languages
- Baselines from current self-supervised speech models
- An evaluation protocol that accounts for tonal, agglutinative, and code-switched features common across the languages in scope

## Findings

Even the strongest current models lag substantially on the languages benchmarked, with per-language word-error rates ranging from acceptable to effectively unusable. We argue this points to data scarcity and modelling assumptions that travel poorly across language families.

## Future work

The benchmark is intended to be extended. Researchers and language-community contributors are encouraged to reach out about adding additional languages and evaluation tasks.

Computer-vision datasets and models for African food.
ChowNet is a research effort building image datasets and recognition models for African cuisine, aimed at improving how vision systems understand food in African contexts.

## What it is

ChowNet is a research project building image datasets and recognition models centred on African food. Vision systems trained predominantly on Western culinary imagery struggle to identify staples, preparations, and presentations common across African kitchens. ChowNet aims to close that gap.

## Why it matters

Food recognition powers nutritional tracking, restaurant-discovery products, agricultural research, and dietary public-health work. When the underlying models don't recognise jollof, fufu, ugali, or injera, those downstream applications fail Africans by default.

## What we're doing

Building a curated, geographically diverse image dataset; training and benchmarking recognition models; and releasing both as open resources for the broader research community.

Building speech and language resources for under-resourced African languages.
Sauti is TRI AI's research initiative focused on speech technology for African languages, building datasets, models, and benchmarks for languages historically excluded from speech AI.

## The challenge

Most modern speech systems perform well on a small number of high-resource languages. The thousands of African languages spoken by hundreds of millions of people remain largely under-served — both because data is scarce and because the linguistic features of many African languages are under-studied in mainstream NLP.

## Our approach

The Sauti Project takes a community-driven approach to building speech resources for under-represented African languages. The work spans three parallel threads.

### Data

Curating, recording, and releasing high-quality speech datasets for languages we work with — often in partnership with local communities, universities, and language activists.

### Models

Training and evaluating speech recognition and synthesis models tuned for the acoustic and linguistic features of the languages in scope.

### Benchmarks

Establishing benchmarks and evaluation protocols that the broader research community can use to make verifiable progress on African-language speech.

## Get involved

Researchers, linguists, native speakers, and community organisers are all welcome to collaborate. Reach out at <research@tri-ai.org>.

A community-led AI study group running cohorts of learners through structured curricula, peer learning, and mentorship.
TRI AI Saturdays Lagos is a community-led learning programme that has run multiple cohorts of African learners through hands-on AI curricula since its founding.

## About the programme

TRI AI Saturdays Lagos is the flagship teaching initiative of TRI AI — a community-led AI study group that has run multiple cohorts of learners through structured curricula since its founding. Each cohort spans roughly twelve weeks and combines self-paced study with weekly meet-ups, mentorship from working AI practitioners, and peer-learning sessions.

## What participants do

- Work through curated AI/ML curricula at their own pace
- Meet weekly in-person or online for discussion, doubt-clearing, and project work
- Pair with mentors drawn from industry and research
- Build a capstone project presented at cohort end

## Who it's for

The programme is open to learners across Nigeria and the wider African continent who have basic programming experience and want to build a foundation in AI. We particularly welcome applicants from groups under-represented in AI.

## Outcomes

Past cohorts have produced graduates working at AI labs, research groups, and product teams across Africa and globally. Many alumni return as mentors for subsequent cohorts.

ChowNet: a new image dataset for African cuisine
TRI AI researchers have released ChowNet, an image dataset and benchmark for recognising African cuisine — a category that is consistently under-represented in mainstream food recognition systems.

TRI AI researchers have released ChowNet, an image dataset and benchmark for recognising African cuisine.

Existing food-recognition datasets contain little African food imagery, leaving downstream applications — from nutrition tools to inventory systems — poorly suited to billions of meals eaten across the continent each year. ChowNet is a first step at addressing that imbalance, with paired baselines on contemporary vision models and a clear path forward for community contributions.

The dataset, code, and accompanying paper are linked from the Publications page. We welcome contributions of additional imagery, annotations, and evaluation tasks.

Applications open for TRI AI Saturdays Lagos — Cohort 9
Applications are now open for the ninth cohort of TRI AI Saturdays Lagos, our flagship community-led learning programme. The cohort will run for twelve weeks beginning April.

Applications are now open for the ninth cohort of TRI AI Saturdays Lagos, our flagship community-led learning programme.

Cohort 9 will run for twelve weeks beginning in April. As with previous cohorts, the programme is free, structured around weekly meet-ups in Lagos, and combines self-paced study with peer learning and mentorship from working AI practitioners.

We particularly encourage applications from final-year students, recent graduates, and self-taught engineers in Nigeria who want a structured route into machine learning and applied AI.

The application form and detail are on the Programmes page.

Sauti Project releases benchmark for African speech recognition
TRI AI's Sauti Project has published a new benchmark for speech recognition across five under-resourced African languages, alongside baseline results from contemporary self-supervised speech models.

The Sauti Project this week released a public benchmark for automatic speech recognition (ASR) across five under-resourced African languages, with paired baseline results from contemporary self-supervised speech models.

The benchmark — and the accompanying paper, published at the African NLP Workshop — is the result of more than a year of dataset construction, evaluation design, and community review. It is intended as a reusable evaluation harness for future African ASR work, not a one-off comparison.

The full paper, evaluation code, and dataset documentation are linked from the Publications page.
